dimensions.note: Credentials are resolved via fromIni({ profile }) only when a non-empty profile is supplied, otherwise the ambient AWS credential chain (apiFromGlobals). No secrets are handled, vaulted, or logged; stackSetName/callAs/region/profile are non-secret globalArguments and the profile name is a plain identifier. No credential material appears in the result resource or in any test fixture. The SSO export-credentials workaround (fromIni does not read the SSO cache) is documented in the README and the module JSDoc.

context.logger.info logs the destructive intent before the call (stackset name, accounts, OUs, regions, retainStacks) and poll progress with operation/status/poll counters; a non-SUCCEEDED terminal status is logged at error level before the throw. Structured placeholders, no secrets. Account ids that appear in logs are operator-supplied delete targets, which is the appropriate audit trail for a destructive operation.

Destructive failures are surfaced, never masked. deleteInstances returning no OperationId throws; a non-SUCCEEDED terminal status writes the result resource (recording the failure honestly) and then throws; the poll timeout throws with the last observed status as context; deleteStackSet lets an SDK error propagate before writing any SUCCEEDED result, so no false success is ever recorded. SDK retries are capped at 8 attempts. Verified by mutation: removing the post-write throw on a non-SUCCEEDED status turns the smoke test red.

Unit tests cover every validateDeleteInstances guard branch (no regions, no targets, whole-OU/root without confirmWholeTarget, accounts under a non-INTERSECTION filter, and the confirmWholeTarget bypass), pollToTerminal reaching a terminal status and throwing on timeout after maxPolls, and isoOrEmpty. The smoke suite drives both run* entrypoints against an in-memory fake LifecycleApi (no live AWS) and asserts the written result's key and full shape plus the guard-short-circuits-before-any-API invariant. Test teeth confirmed by six independent source mutations (guard always-null, terminal set weakened, timeout swallowed, result key broken, INTERSECTION gate dropped, post-write throw removed) — all six turn a test red. Thin-variant gaps remain (an explicit STOPPED-throws path, the abort-signal path in delay(), deleteStackSet throwing) but each is a variant of already-covered logic; non-blocking.

DeleteStackInstances and DeleteStackSet are naturally tolerant of already-deleted targets (AWS returns success or a benign error), so a re-run after a partial retirement is safe. Each run writes a fresh result keyed by operation id (delete-instances) or stackset name (delete-stackset), so re-runs do not clobber prior records and garbageCollection retains the last 20. The poll loop re-reads operation status idempotently and holds no shared mutable client-side state.

The LifecycleApi facade maps cleanly to the three real CloudFormation calls. DeploymentTargets correctly converts empty OU/account arrays to undefined so the API is never sent empty arrays; RetainStacks, CallAs, and Regions are threaded verbatim; describeOperation defaults a missing status to 'UNKNOWN' rather than silently SUCCEEDED. The public DeleteInstancesArgs interface mirrors the internal DeleteInstancesArgsSchema field-for-field with no drift, so the doc-lint seam cannot admit an invalid arg or reject a valid one. deleteStackSet correctly declares NO method arguments: DeleteStackSet returns no StackSet operation to poll, so the previously vestigial pollSeconds/maxPolls (which execute() ignored) were removed and the module/README/manifest descriptions were corrected so only deleteInstances is described as polling — accepted args now match observable behavior. The abort signal from context.signal is threaded through.

A single CloudFormationClient is created per execution; the AWS SDK client needs no explicit teardown. delay() clears its timeout and rejects on abort, removes its 'abort' listener on normal completion, and short-circuits an already-aborted signal before scheduling — so a shared context.signal does not accumulate one dead listener per poll iteration (no MaxListenersExceededWarning) and no timer leaks across a cancelled run. No file handles, sockets, or temp files are opened. The smoke suite includes a sentinel asserting each test finishes well under a network budget, guarding against accidental real I/O. The AbortSignal is propagated for prompt cancellation.

The exported surface is intentional: the pure helpers (validateDeleteInstances, isoOrEmpty, pollToTerminal), the LifecycleApi/DeleteInstancesInput facade types, the run* entrypoints plus their *Deps, DeleteInstancesArgs, LifecycleResult, and model. No private or SDK types leak through an exported signature (the hand-written DeleteInstancesArgs seam exists precisely to avoid exporting z.infer of a private schema); deno doc --lint is clean. grep -rniE 'sitoo|vanta|aikido|[0-9]{12}|jentz@|m@s\.' over the whole extension directory returns only synthetic RFC-style placeholder account ids (111111111111/222222222222) in the README and tests and the /^[0-9]{12}$/ account-id validation regex — the prototype's VantaOrgSetup/vanta-auditor identifiers and the eu-west-1-homing/migration narrative were genericized to ExampleOrgBaseline. No real account ids, vendor names, internal hostnames, or emails remain. (Nit: isoOrEmpty is exported and unit-tested but currently unused on the production path; retained because it is an acceptance-criteria-mandated pure helper and wiring it in would require expanding the describeOperation contract.)

ResultSchema is a plain strict z.object — the prototype's trailing .passthrough() was removed during extraction to match the aws-stackset-audit sibling and the repo's schema-strictness convention (confirmed by grep: no .passthrough() anywhere in the extension). The model writes exactly the explicit LifecycleResult shape, which the schema mirrors field-for-field, so tightening loses nothing. All argument schemas (GlobalArgsSchema, DeleteInstancesArgsSchema) are strict z.object, and DeleteStackSetArgsSchema is an explicit empty z.object since the method takes no arguments. The public resource type LifecycleResult is a hand-written interface rather than z.infer of a private schema, keeping deno doc --lint clean.

The single result resource declares lifetime 'infinite' with garbageCollection 20 — a deliberate choice (the read-only audit sibling uses 10) documented in the README, appropriate for retaining the audit trail across a multi-batch retirement.

Deliberately delete-only by design: this is the write-side sibling. Create/update are out of scope (the official @swamp/aws Cloud Control stack-set type owns them) and list/describe belong to the read-only audit sibling. deleteInstances (batched, with the safety guard and operation polling) and deleteStackSet (the final empty-set delete) compose a complete staged-retirement lifecycle, and the README 'Out of scope' section documents the boundary.

validateDeleteInstances runs before any destructive API call — a mutation flipping the guard to always-pass makes the smoke test's calls.deleteInstances.length === 0 and written.length === 0 assertions fail, proving the short-circuit. The INTERSECTION+accounts gate correctly bounds blast radius: under AWS DeploymentTargets semantics INTERSECTION can only narrow the explicit accounts list, so a broad OU or the org root cannot widen the target set; whole-OU/root targets and accounts under UNION/NONE/DIFFERENCE are refused unless confirmWholeTarget=true. No under-rejection hole was found, and the documented safe shape is not falsely rejected.

Mechanical check: result keys are deterministic and collision-resistant — delete-instances-<operationId> (unique per AWS StackSet operation) and delete-stackset-<stackSetName>. The two methods cannot collide, and each is written by a single code path. Both keys are asserted exactly in the smoke suite, and the CEL access patterns are documented in the README.

Each method writes exactly one result via context.writeResource and returns {...result, dataHandles: [handle]}; nothing is read back from stored swamp state (no readResource), consistent with a fire-and-record mutating operation. The written object matches ResultSchema/LifecycleResult field-for-field, and the smoke test asserts the spec name 'result' and dataHandles.length === 1.

Initial publish. model.upgrades carries the required single no-op entry { toVersion: '2026.06.17.1', description: 'Initial publish', upgradeAttributes: (old) => old } whose toVersion equals model.version equals the manifest version (2026.06.17.1); the colocated entrypoint test asserts this invariant. The chain was added during extraction — the prototype had none — establishing a clean base for future schema-changing upgrades.
